summary: Denver's verified affordable-housing brief now rests on direct source fetches for Expanding Housing Affordability, dedicated affordable-housing revenues, HOST and All In Mile High, Denver Housing Authority redevelopment, and recent CHFA tax-credit awards. Unsupported NEST, Proposition 123, stale election-result, and blocked-source claims were stripped.

paragraphs.text: Denver's Expanding Housing Affordability ordinance became effective July 1, 2022. The city page says EHA updated the 2017 affordable-housing linkage fee and created new affordable-housing requirements for residential developments of 10 or more dwelling units; the March 2022 policy approach describes the paired mandatory-housing and linkage-fee framework.

The retained EHA on-site requirements are those directly shown by the city page and policy PDF: new residential development of 10 or more units must build affordable units or use an alternative compliance option; baseline requirements include 8% of units at 60% AMI in typical market areas, 10% at 60% AMI in high market areas, 12% averaging 70% AMI in typical market areas, and 15% averaging 70% AMI in high market areas; income-restricted units have a 99-year affordability term.

The linkage-fee side applies to development outside the mandatory-affordable-housing trigger, including residential projects of nine or fewer units and nonresidential development. The city page says linkage fees adjust annually and lists a July 1, 2025 rate of $8.00 per square foot for larger single-, two-, or multi-unit residential projects of nine units or fewer; the city GFA guide explains that 2022 linkage-fee projects use International Building Code gross floor area, and Mile High CRE reported the July 2025 increase.

BusinessDen reported that U.S. District Judge Philip Brimmer dismissed RedT Homes' federal challenge to Denver's income-restricted housing requirements on March 3, 2026, without prejudice, while describing the challenged fee as part of Denver's income-restricted housing program.

Denver's Dedicated Affordable Housing Fund page documents the 2016 fund-creation process, including September 19 final council approval, the two-source revenue structure of a 0.5-mill property tax and development impact fees, an estimated $150 million over the first 10 years, and a 6,000-unit production or preservation target.

Denver later added marijuana revenue to the housing stack. FOX31 reported that Mayor Hancock signed an ordinance increasing the local marijuana tax from 3.5% to 5.5% for affordable housing, while Westword later reported that the 2-percentage-point increase produced nearly $38.8 million from late 2018 through 2022 and helped fund HOST land acquisition, unit development, housing assistance, and supportive services.

sections.heading: HOST and All In Mile High

paragraphs.text: The Department of Housing Stability page describes HOST as Denver's housing and homelessness department, and the 2025 HOST Action Plan says the department planned roughly $229 million in affordable-housing and homelessness-resolution investments in 2025, including All In Mile High, rent and utility assistance, and shelter expansion budget items.

The Mayor's All In Mile High page says Mayor Mike Johnston signed an emergency declaration on unsheltered homelessness on July 18, 2023, set the House1000 goal of moving 1,000 people indoors by year-end, met that goal by the end of 2023, and launched All In Mile High in 2024 with a 2,000-people-indoors goal that was surpassed.

Denver's 2025 All In Mile High report says more than 7,000 people came inside from the streets and more than 5,597 moved into permanent housing since 2023. The Urban Institute interim evaluation describes All In Mile High as a citywide effort using encampment resolution, noncongregate shelters with services, permanent housing connections, rapid rehousing, and supportive housing.

Denver Housing Authority and city releases say the September 2025 Flo opening completed the seven-building Sun Valley redevelopment: Flo added 212 apartments for adults 55 and older and people with disabilities, and the redevelopment replaced 333 outdated public-housing units with 965 mixed-income homes serving households from 20% to 100% AMI. The funding stack includes a $30 million HUD Choice Neighborhoods Implementation grant, more than $60 million through DHA Delivers for Denver, and more than $500 million in new development.

DHA's Mariposa page describes the Mariposa District redevelopment as a $197 million, 14-acre effort near the Osage and 10th light-rail station, expected to add 581 multifamily mixed-income units while avoiding displacement of existing affordable-housing residents.

For future DHA pipeline, Developing MyCity reports that DHA is exploring Westridge and Mulroy redevelopment near Knox Station, with a first phase of 133 units and a broader concept that could support roughly 600 to 800 affordable units across the properties.

CHFA's 2024 Round One announcement lists four Denver awards retained here: Beeler Park Flats with Mile High Development, Iliff Senior Apartments with MGL Partners, Kappa Tower III with Kappa Housing, and Vina Senior Residences with CV Colorado.
CHFA's 2024 Round Two announcement lists Creekside Flats in Denver with Archway Investment Corporation as developer among federal 4% and state Housing Tax Credit award reservations.
CHFA's 2025 Round One announcement lists three retained Denver awards: Park Avenue Apartments, Rita Bass Apartments, and Tapestry LIHTC.
CHFA's 2025 Round Two announcement lists five retained Denver awards: 1001 Lincoln Street, 1139 Delaware Street, Central Park Station Phase I, Cole Train, and University Building Lofts.
